Row1.Rationale: Nintedanib competitively binds ATP sites of VEGFR‑1/2/3, producing sustained VEGFR‑2 blockade (>30–32 h), reducing endothelial and perivascular cell proliferation, microvessel density, vessel permeability and perfusion in NSCLC xenografts, leading to tumor growth arrest and enhanced efficacy with docetaxel or pemetrexed.

Row2.Rationale: As a potent inhibitor of FGFR‑1/2/3, nintedanib suppresses FGF‑driven endothelial, fibroblast and cancer cell proliferation; FGFR signaling is a documented escape route after VEGF blockade, and triple angiokinase inhibition prevents this compensatory pathway, contributing to antitumor and anti‑fibrotic activity and overcoming resistance to prior anti‑VEGF therapy.

Row3.Rationale: Nintedanib inhibits PDGFR‑α/β on pericytes, vascular smooth‑muscle cells and PDGFRA‑amplified NSCLC cells (NCI‑H1703), reducing vessel stabilization and stromal support; in NSCLC, greater on‑treatment reductions in circulating PDGF correlate with longer PFS and OS on nintedanib–docetaxel, supporting PDGFR pathway suppression as a driver of benefit.

Row4.Rationale: In angiogenesis‑relevant cells and PDGF/FGF‑driven tumor lines, nintedanib decreases phosphorylation of AKT downstream of VEGFR/FGFR/PDGFR, inducing proliferation arrest and apoptosis; in NSCLC xenografts, triple angiokinase inhibition reduces PI3K–AKT signaling together with vascular regression, contributing to tumor shrinkage and chemosensitization.

Row5.Rationale: Nintedanib suppresses MAPK/ERK phosphorylation in PDGFRA‑amplified NCI‑H1703 and FGFR2‑amplified KatoIII cells, with associated apoptosis (cleaved PARP, caspase‑3); in NSCLC xenografts, inhibition of VEGFR/FGFR/PDGFR signaling leads to reduced MAPK activity and tumor cell proliferation, supporting MAPK downregulation as a key antiproliferative mechanism.

Row6.Rationale: In NSCLC xenografts, nintedanib did not increase EMT markers despite potent anti‑angiogenic pressure, unlike other VEGF‑targeted agents; siRNA/compound studies show nintedanib downregulates mesenchymal genes SYDE1 and ZEB1 and induces E‑cadherin, reversing EMT across lung (A549) and other carcinoma models, which may counter EMT‑mediated drug resistance.

Row7.Rationale: Anti‑VEGF monotherapies drive compensatory upregulation of FGF and PDGF in NSCLC, mediating resistance; by simultaneously inhibiting VEGFR, FGFR and PDGFR, nintedanib functionally downregulates pro‑angiogenic signaling despite potential ligand upregulation, limiting hypoxia‑driven escape and sustaining anti‑tumor angiogenesis blockade.

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: Extracellular matrix / fibroblast activation and fibrosis
Row8.Rationale: Nintedanib blocks VEGFR/FGFR/PDGFR on lung fibroblasts and myofibroblasts, inhibiting proliferation, migration and transformation, and reduces collagen deposition and fibrosis in multiple lung models; remodeling of a stiff, pro‑angiogenic fibrotic stroma likely improves drug penetration and reduces pro‑tumorigenic signaling in lung adenocarcinoma.

Row9.Rationale: In syngeneic models, nintedanib plus anti‑PD‑1 produces greater tumor inhibition than either alone, including in PD‑1‑refractory tumors; nintedanib normalizes vasculature, reduces immunosuppressive fibroblasts, increases CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration and, in combination with RT, further upregulates PD‑L1 and enhances response to anti‑PD‑L1, supporting synergy with ICIs.

Row10.Rationale: Nintedanib suppresses radiation‑induced ATF4 and downstream SLC7A11‑mediated glutathione synthesis, lowering antioxidant capacity and promoting lipid peroxidation and ferroptosis; in A549 and other tumor models this enhances radiosensitivity and antitumor efficacy of RT, with increased T‑cell infiltration and no major added toxicity.

Row11.Pathway ID/Name: Direct oncogenic RTKs (PDGFRA‑amplified, FGFR‑altered, RET‑fusion NSCLC)
Row11.Rationale: Kinome and cell‑line screens show nintedanib potently inhibits PDGFRA‑amplified NCI‑H1703 and RET‑rearranged LC‑2/ad NSCLC cells, reducing MAPK/AKT signaling and inducing tumor shrinkage in NCI‑H1703 xenografts, indicating that, beyond stromal angiogenesis, nintedanib can directly target oncogenic RTKs in defined NSCLC subsets.

Row12.Rationale: VEGF, FGF and PDGF promote immunosuppressive TMEs by impairing dendritic cells, expanding Tregs and MDSCs and limiting T‑cell trafficking; by blocking these pathways, nintedanib is proposed to convert “cold” to “hot” tumors and has shown preclinical synergy with PD‑1/PD‑L1 blockade and clinical exploration in NSCLC (e.g., nintedanib–pembrolizumab trials).

Row13.Rationale: Nintedanib competitively inhibits VEGFR‑1/2/3, producing sustained VEGFR2 blockade, which reduces endothelial and perivascular cell proliferation, microvessel density, vascular permeability and perfusion in NSCLC models, leading to tumor growth inhibition and enhanced activity of docetaxel or pemetrexed.

Row14.Rationale: By targeting FGFR‑1/2/3, nintedanib suppresses FGF‑driven proliferation of endothelial cells, fibroblasts and some tumor cells; FGF signaling is a key escape route after VEGF‑only inhibition, so triple angiokinase blockade helps prevent compensatory angiogenesis and stromal support in NSCLC.

Row15.Rationale: Nintedanib inhibits PDGFR‑α/β on pericytes, vascular smooth‑muscle cells and PDGFRA‑amplified NSCLC cells, impairing vessel stabilization and stromal proliferation; suppression of PDGF signaling contributes to anti‑angiogenic efficacy and may correlate with longer PFS/OS in nintedanib‑treated NSCLC.

Row16.Rationale: Separate inhibition of VEGFR, FGFR or PDGFR has limited durability because tumors upregulate alternative pro‑angiogenic ligands; nintedanib was designed to bind the ATP pocket of all three RTK families, enabling simultaneous blockade of redundant angiogenesis and fibrotic pathways that sustain NSCLC vasculature.

Row17.Rationale: VEGFR, FGFR and PDGFR converge on PI3K–AKT to promote endothelial and fibroblast survival, EMT and stromal activation; nintedanib reduces AKT phosphorylation by blocking these upstream RTKs, but parallel oncogenic inputs (e.g., KRAS, other cytokine receptors) can maintain AKT signaling and contribute to incomplete responses.

Row18.Rationale: In endothelial, fibroblast and RTK‑addicted tumor cells, nintedanib decreases MAPK/ERK phosphorylation downstream of VEGFR/FGFR/PDGFR, leading to reduced proliferation and increased apoptosis; in NSCLC xenografts this correlates with vascular regression and tumor growth delay.

Row19.Rationale: VEGF/FGF/PDGF and PI3K–AKT/MAPK signaling support EMT and myofibroblast/CAF phenotypes; nintedanib shows anti‑fibrotic effects on lung adenocarcinoma‑derived fibroblasts and can reverse EMT‑like gene expression in preclinical models, but EMT can also be maintained by TGF‑β and Wnt, providing residual resistance.

Row20.Rationale: In lung fibrosis and cancer, TGF‑β/Smad drives EMT, EndMT and myofibroblast differentiation; by inhibiting VEGFR/FGFR/PDGFR on fibroblasts and stromal cells, nintedanib indirectly dampens TGF‑β–dependent activation and collagen deposition, helping remodel the fibrotic, pro‑tumorigenic niche in lung adenocarcinoma.

Row21.Pathway ID/Name: Extracellular matrix / fibroblast activation and fibrosis
Row21.Rationale: Nintedanib reduces proliferation, migration and activation of lung fibroblasts and cancer‑associated fibroblasts via RTK blockade, leading to decreased extracellular‑matrix production and fibrosis; this “defibrotic” effect can normalize the tumor stroma, improve drug delivery and reduce pro‑angiogenic and pro‑invasive signaling.

Row22.Rationale: Anti‑angiogenic therapy can exacerbate hypoxia and stabilize HIF‑1α, increasing VEGF and other pro‑angiogenic ligands; although nintedanib blocks VEGFR/FGFR/PDGFR signaling, persistent hypoxia‑induced factors and alternative receptors may support adaptive resistance and limit the duration of benefit.

Row23.Rationale: VEGF/FGF/PDGF signaling promotes an immunosuppressive microenvironment by impairing dendritic cells, expanding Tregs/MDSCs and restricting T‑cell trafficking; by normalizing vessels and attenuating stromal activation, nintedanib is proposed to enhance T‑cell infiltration and synergize with chemotherapy and PD‑1/PD‑L1 blockade in NSCLC.

Row24.Rationale: Beyond stromal angiogenesis, nintedanib inhibits oncogenic RTKs such as FGFRs, PDGFRA and RET; in RTK‑addicted lung cancer models this reduces MAPK/AKT signaling and tumor growth, suggesting potential direct antitumor effects in FGFR‑ or RET‑driven NSCLC subsets, although this is not an approved biomarker strategy.
